local_names.romanized_local: Nèn dòufu (Mandarin); Kinugoshi tofu (Japanese); Tauhu sutera (Malay)

aliases: 嫩豆腐 (Nèn dòufu) / 软豆腐 (Ruǎn dòufu) / 滑豆腐 (Huá dòufu)

chinese: 嫩豆腐 (Nèn dòufu) / 软豆腐 (Ruǎn dòufu) / 滑豆腐 (Huá dòufu)

cuisine: Chinese / Japanese / Pan-Asian (widely used in Singapore cooking)
region_of_origin: China (tofu origin ~2nd century BCE, Han Dynasty); silken/kinugoshi texture method developed in Japan
food_subgroup: Silken/soft tofu — unpressed, high-water-content, custard-like texture; coagulated with calcium sulfate (Chinese-style) or GDL/nigari (Japanese-style); distinct from firm tofu which has higher protein/fat density (~8g protein/100g)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI distinguishing silken vs firm vs extra-firm tofu. Using parent 'tofu (food)' FOODON_03302196 as closest_parent. Silken-specific subclass not available in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229892004 covers tofu generically; no silken-specific descendant code confirmed.

preparation_method: coagulated with calcium sulfate or glucono delta-lactone (GDL) or nigari (magnesium chloride)
no heat treatment after coagulation for cold preparations
main_ingredients: coagulant (calcium sulfate or GDL or nigari)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical Singapore serving of silken tofu is approximately 150g (roughly one small block), providing about 83 kcal, 7.5g protein, 4.5g fat, and 270mg calcium.

nutrient_source.title: Tofu, soft, prepared with calcium sulfate — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 16165 / FDC ID 172449)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy NDB 16165 'Tofu, soft, prepared with calcium sulfate' is an exact match for calcium sulfate-set silken/soft tofu. Values cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo for Singapore-market relevance. Calcium value (180mg/100g) reflects calcium sulfate coagulant contribution, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database (tofu / soy protein foods)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~25 for soft/silken tofu is consistent with published values for soy-based tofu in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Tofu is protein-dominant with very low available carbohydrate (~1.5–2g/100g); GI is low by both direct measurement and analogy with firm tofu (GI ~15). GL per 150g serving = (25 × 2.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.75, rounded to 0.6 for 100g reference. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Near-zero sodium (8mg/100g) and modest potassium content (120mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Silken tofu is an excellent choice for hypertensive patients with only 8mg sodium per 100g; soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) may provide modest additional antihypertensive benefit, and the potassium content supports favourable sodium-potassium balance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and negligible glycemic load (~0.4 per 100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Silken tofu has a GI of approximately 25 and a GL of ~0.4 per 100g, making it a negligible contributor to postprandial glucose; soy protein replaces higher-glycaemic protein sources and soy isoflavones (particularly daidzein) may improve insulin sensitivity in type 2 diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.4g/100g), zero cholesterol, favourable PUFA profile, and soy isoflavones
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Silken tofu contains only 0.4g saturated fat and 0mg cholesterol per 100g, with a polyunsaturated-fat-dominant lipid profile; regular soy protein consumption is associated with modest LDL-cholesterol reduction, and soy isoflavones may provide additional cardioprotective effects.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High calcium (180mg/100g) and anti-inflammatory PUFA profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Silken tofu provides 180mg calcium per 100g (important for RA patients on long-term glucocorticoids at risk of steroid-induced osteoporosis), very low SFA, and an anti-inflammatory PUFA and isoflavone profile.

osteoporosis.key_factor: High calcium content (180mg/100g) from calcium sulfate coagulant
osteoporosis.guidance: Calcium sulfate-set silken tofu provides 180mg calcium per 100g — among the highest of plant-based protein foods — making it a clinically useful dairy-free calcium source for osteoporosis prevention and management.

dysphagia.key_factor: IDDSI Level 6 (soft and bite-sized) and Level 4 (pureed) compatible texture
dysphagia.guidance: Silken tofu's custard-like, unpressed texture is suitable for IDDSI Level 6 (soft and bite-sized) and can be blended to Level 4 (pureed), making it a valuable high-protein, high-calcium food for patients on dysphagia diets in hospital and community settings.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Silken tofu (soft/silken grade, undrained) is an unpressed, high-water-content soy curd with a smooth, custard-like texture produced by coagulating hot soy milk without draining the whey. In Chinese-style production, calcium sulfate is used as the coagulant; in Japanese-style (kinugoshi), glucono delta-lactone (GDL) or nigari (magnesium chloride) is used. Per 100g, silken tofu provides approximately 55 kcal, 5g protein, 3g fat (only 0.4g saturated), 2g carbohydrate, 0.4g dietary fibre, and a notably high 180mg calcium — among the highest of any plant-based protein food, attributable to the calcium sulfate coagulant. Sodium is near-zero at 8mg/100g. Glycemic index is approximately 25 (low), with a negligible glycemic load of ~0.4 per 100g, making it suitable for diabetes management. Silken tofu is VEGAN, Halal, Kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is classified as NOVA Class 1 (minimally processed). In Singapore, silken tofu is one of the most widely consumed plant proteins, appearing in hawker cold tofu dishes, mapo tofu, Japanese hiyayakko, hospital catering, and dysphagia diets (IDDSI Level 6/4 compatible). Soy isoflavones (~25mg/100g, primarily genistein and daidzein) contribute anti-inflammatory, modest antihypertensive, and potential insulin-sensitising effects. No clinically significant warfarin interaction has been established at typical dietary intake levels. Source: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 16165 and HPB Singapore Foodo.
common_user_queries: is silken tofu good for diabetes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 16165 ('Tofu, soft, prepared with calcium sulfate') — an exact match for calcium sulfate-set silken/soft tofu — cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are supported by this approved primary source. GI value (~25) is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature for soy-based tofu. Confidence is HIGH.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0mg/100g) — silken tofu is a plant-derived food containing no dietary cholesterol; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 16165. Vitamin A, vitamin C, vitamin D, and vitamin B12 are confirmed zero for plain silken tofu per USDA SR Legacy; these are naturally absent in unsupplemented soy curd. Trans fat is 0.0g — naturally zero in plant-based minimally processed soy products. Monounsaturated fat (0.7g) and polyunsaturated fat (1.7g) values are from USDA SR Legacy NDB 16165 fatty acid profile for soft tofu. Water content (87.8g/100g) from USDA SR Legacy. Calcium value of 180mg/100g reflects calcium sulfate coagulant contribution and is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for calcium-sulfate-set tofu; GDL-set or nigari-set silken tofu will have lower calcium (~30–60mg/100g). GI ~25 is an analogous estimate consistent with University of Sydney GI Database values for soy tofu; direct GI measurement for silken tofu specifically may vary slightly. Soy isoflavone content (~25mg/100g) is noted for clinical context but is not a standard USDA nutrient field. IDDSI Level 6/4 suitability noted for clinical dietetics use. No warfarin interaction at typical dietary intake. NOVA Class 1 classification applies to plain silken tofu without additives.
